items.question: What is a loss surface in machine learning?
options: The plot of accuracy vs epoch
The graph of L(θ) over parameters θ
items.answer: The graph of L(θ) over parameters θ
items.explanation: A loss surface assigns each parameter vector θ a height L(θ); optimization moves downhill on this geometry.

items.question: For a convex loss surface, a local minimum is always:
items.explanation: Convexity means any local bowl is the unique global bowl; no separate lower valley exists elsewhere.

items.question: A saddle point on the loss surface has:
options: Zero gradient and loss lower in all directions
Zero gradient but loss decreases in some directions and increases in others
items.answer: Zero gradient but loss decreases in some directions and increases in others
items.explanation: Saddles are critical points (∇L = 0) that are not minima; curvature is positive along some axes and negative along others.

items.question: A 2D contour plot of loss most directly shows:
options: Level curves of equal L(θ) in a parameter plane
items.answer: Level curves of equal L(θ) in a parameter plane
items.explanation: Each contour connects (θ₁, θ₂) pairs with the same loss value—a top-down slice of the surface.

items.question: Why can two runs with different initializations reach different test accuracy despite similar training loss?
options: The loss function changes between runs
Non-convex surfaces have multiple basins; different paths can land in valleys with different generalization
Gradients are undefined on loss surfaces
items.answer: Non-convex surfaces have multiple basins; different paths can land in valleys with different generalization
items.explanation: On non-convex landscapes, initialization determines which basin you enter; valley shape affects generalization beyond training loss depth.
